quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Conservation of mechanical energy"?

options.text: F_net = ma or F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals mass times acceleration, or equivalently the rate of change of momentum
A mass on a string tracing a horizontal circle; T sinθ provides centripetal force while T cosθ balances gravity
KE₁ + PE₁ = KE₂ + PE₂; total mechanical energy is constant when only conservative forces do work
A quantity with both magnitude and direction, represented as an arrow; examples include displacement, velocity, force, and momentum

quiz_questions.explanation: "Conservation of mechanical energy" is defined as: KE₁ + PE₁ = KE₂ + PE₂; total mechanical energy is constant when only conservative forces do work. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Mechanical energy"?

options.text: The contact force perpendicular to a surface, adjusting to prevent objects from passing through each other
F_avg = Δp/Δt = J/Δt; the constant force that would deliver the same impulse over the same time interval
The pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, or cable; in an ideal (massless) string, tension is the same throughout
E_mech = KE + PE; the sum of kinetic and potential energies of a system

quiz_questions.explanation: "Mechanical energy" is defined as: E_mech = KE + PE; the sum of kinetic and potential energies of a system. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Non-conservative work"?

options.text: f_s ≤ μ_s N; opposes the tendency of motion between surfaces in contact; self-adjusting up to the maximum value μ_s N
A×B; a vector perpendicular to both A and B with magnitude ABsinθ; used in calculating torque and magnetic force
ΔKE = KE_f - KE_i; negative for inelastic collisions; equals -½μv_rel² where μ = m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂) is the reduced mass
W_nc = ΔKE + ΔPE = E_final - E_initial; work by friction or applied forces that changes the total mechanical energy

quiz_questions.explanation: "Non-conservative work" is defined as: W_nc = ΔKE + ΔPE = E_final - E_initial; work by friction or applied forces that changes the total mechanical energy. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Energy dissipation"?

options.text: Conversion of mechanical energy to thermal energy by friction: ΔE_thermal = f_k × d
v(t) = dx/dt; the derivative of position with respect to time, giving the rate and direction of motion at each instant
KE_rot = ½Iω²; energy due to rotation, analogous to ½mv² for translation
f_s ≤ μ_s N; opposes the tendency of motion between surfaces in contact; self-adjusting up to the maximum value μ_s N

quiz_questions.explanation: "Energy dissipation" is defined as: Conversion of mechanical energy to thermal energy by friction: ΔE_thermal = f_k × d. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Energy bar charts"?

options.text: Visual representations showing the distribution of energy (KE, PE, thermal) at different points in a process
KE_rot = ½Iω²; energy due to rotation, analogous to ½mv² for translation
The net impulse on an object equals its change in momentum: J_net = p_final - p_initial
W = ∫F·dr; the scalar integral of force along displacement; measured in joules (J); positive when force and displacement are in the same direction

quiz_questions.explanation: "Energy bar charts" is defined as: Visual representations showing the distribution of energy (KE, PE, thermal) at different points in a process. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "KE₁ + PE₁ = KE₂ + PE₂; total mechanical energy is constant when only conservative forces do work"?

options.text: Conservation of angular momentum
Net force
Conservation of mechanical energy
Critical speed

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Conservation of mechanical energy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "E_mech = KE + PE; the sum of kinetic and potential energies of a system"?

options.text: Parallel axis theorem
Mechanical energy
Power
Energy in pendulum

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Mechanical energy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "W_nc = ΔKE + ΔPE = E_final - E_initial; work by friction or applied forces that changes the total mechanical energy"?

options.text: Coefficient of restitution
Non-conservative work
Elastic PE
Force-time graph

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Non-conservative work".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Conversion of mechanical energy to thermal energy by friction: ΔE_thermal = f_k × d"?

options.text: Energy dissipation
Restoring torque
Friction as a non-conservative force
Non-uniform circular motion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Energy dissipation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Visual representations showing the distribution of energy (KE, PE, thermal) at different points in a process"?

options.text: Transverse vs. longitudinal
Horsepower
Energy bar charts
Instantaneous power

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Energy bar charts".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Conservation of Energy is accurate?

options.text: The critical condition at the top of a vertical loop (minimum speed for maintaining contact) occurs when the normal force equals zero: mg = mv²/r, giving v_min = √(gr).
The parallel axis theorem I = I_cm + Md² relates the moment of inertia about any axis to that about the center of mass.
For an isolated system, the center of mass velocity is constant regardless of internal interactions, making it invaluable for analyzing explosions and multi-body systems.
Conservation of Energy states that in an isolated system, total mechanical energy (KE + PE) remains constant when only conservative forces do work: KE₁ + PE₁ = KE₂ + PE₂.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Conservation of Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Conservation of Energy?

options.text: Energy conservation with U = -GMm/r is essential for orbit analysis: total mechanical energy E = ½mv² - GMm/r determines whether an orbit is bound (E < 0) or unbound (E ≥ 0), and escape velocity follows from setting E = 0.
For variable forces (such as springs: F = -kx), integration is essential: W_spring = ∫(-kx)dx = -½kx².
When non-conservative forces (friction, air resistance) are present, the generalized equation becomes KE₁ + PE₁ + W_nc = KE₂ + PE₂, where W_nc represents energy converted to or from thermal energy.
For an isolated system, the center of mass velocity is constant regardless of internal interactions, making it invaluable for analyzing explosions and multi-body systems.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Conservation of Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Conservation of Energy?

options.text: Torque and Rotational Dynamics extend Newton's Second Law to rotation: τ_net = Iα, where torque τ = r × F (magnitude rF sinθ) is the rotational analog of force, and I (moment of inertia) is the rotational analog of mass.
Power is defined as P = dW/dt = F·v (in watts), connecting force, velocity, and energy transfer rate.
Energy conservation is the most broadly applicable principle in physics.
Collisions are classified by whether kinetic energy is conserved.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Conservation of Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Conservation of Energy?

options.text: The impulse-momentum theorem (J = ∫F dt = Δp) connects the time integral of force to the change in momentum, making it especially useful for analyzing collisions and impacts.
Conservation of Energy states that in an isolated system, total mechanical energy (KE + PE) remains constant when only conservative forces do work: KE₁ + PE₁ = KE₂ + PE₂.
Vectors and Components provide the mathematical language for describing physical quantities with both magnitude and direction.
In vertical circles, the net centripetal force changes with position because gravity's radial component varies.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Conservation of Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Conservation of Energy?

options.text: This formula (with U = 0 at r = ∞) replaces the approximate U = mgh valid only near Earth's surface.
Dynamics problems in AP Physics C require setting up and solving differential equations when forces depend on velocity or position.
Gravitational Potential Energy in the universal form is U = -GMm/r, where r is the distance between centers of mass.
When non-conservative forces (friction, air resistance) are present, the generalized equation becomes KE₁ + PE₁ + W_nc = KE₂ + PE₂, where W_nc represents energy converted to or from thermal energy.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Conservation of Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Conservation of Energy?

options.text: Hydroelectric dam design converting gravitational PE of water (mgh) to kinetic energy of turbines to electrical energy, analyzing efficiency at each conversion stage
Automotive airbag design increasing collision time to reduce peak force on occupants while the same impulse (momentum change) occurs
Wrench design and usage: longer handles provide greater torque for the same applied force, following τ = rF
Orbital mechanics where gravitational force provides the centripetal force: mg = mv²/r leads to orbital velocity v = √(gr) for low orbits

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Conservation of Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Conservation of Energy?

options.text: Molecular chemistry modeling interatomic bonds as potential energy curves (Lennard-Jones potential) to predict bond length and bond energy
Forensic physics reconstructing car accident speeds from skid mark lengths using W_friction = ΔKE to calculate the initial velocity
Wrench design and usage: longer handles provide greater torque for the same applied force, following τ = rF
GPS satellite corrections accounting for the variation of g with altitude to maintain precise timing and positioning

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Conservation of Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Conservation of Energy applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Pole vaulting biomechanics analyzing the conversion of kinetic energy → elastic PE in the pole → gravitational PE at peak height
Flywheel energy storage systems using high-I, high-ω wheels to store kinetic energy for regenerative braking in vehicles and power grid stabilization
Automotive crash testing measuring force-time profiles with accelerometers to calculate impulse and evaluate occupant protection systems
Wrench design and usage: longer handles provide greater torque for the same applied force, following τ = rF

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Conservation of Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Conservation of Energy?

options.text: Civil engineering analyzing dam stability using gravitational potential energy and identifying stable vs. unstable equilibrium configurations
Stellar evolution: collapsing gas clouds spin faster as they shrink (I decreases → ω increases), explaining the rapid rotation of neutron stars
Hydroelectric dam design converting gravitational PE of water (mgh) to kinetic energy of turbines to electrical energy, analyzing efficiency at each conversion stage
Computer graphics and game physics engines using dot and cross products for collision detection, lighting calculations, and surface normal computations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Conservation of Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Conservation of Energy in practice?

options.text: Figure skating spins: pulling arms in reduces I, increasing ω to conserve angular momentum (demonstrating I's physical significance)
Automotive engineering designing braking systems by analyzing deceleration forces and stopping distances using F = ma
Forensic physics reconstructing car accident speeds from skid mark lengths using W_friction = ΔKE to calculate the initial velocity
Earthquake engineering analyzing seismic wave propagation (P-waves longitudinal, S-waves transverse) through different geological layers

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Conservation of Energy.
